CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The reign of mob terror has so developed in Belfast
that Harland and Wolff’s have to announce a partial
closing of their works. That great shipbuilding firm
issued the following statement on Saturday last:—

Matters have now arrived at a crisis in Belfast
ghipyards. Owing to the disturbances the impossi-
bility of carrying on work properly has been growing
daily more obvious. - In view-of the brutal assaults
on individual workmen and the intimidation of
others several departments in Harland and Wolff’s
have already been closed down, and, in their utter
ignorance. that their own interests are affected by
their folly, extremists have gone so far as to molest
and intimidate the specially skilled men respon-
sible for the working of the power plant.  These
men assaulted and intimidated are gradually leav-
ing off.work, and as they cannot be replaced the firm
are reluctantly obliged to shut down a considerable

. portion of the plant, which will affect a still larger

portion of the works, and thus gradually the whole
e'sii{léblis'hmer\t will automatically come to & stand-
stil i’ :

=

Where are the denunciations of the Belfast atrocities
from English and Irish Unionists? These atrocities
are going on and Catholic households—-owing to the
breadwinners being unable to follow their work—are
in dire distress, but the Unionists of Ireland and * Irish
Times,”’ and the non-Catholics in Dublin and in the
““ South and West,”” who are fat on Catholic business
and custom, utter no protest. Were these things
going on in Bulgaria or Africa the non-Catholic Syn
or other organised bodies of Protestants would, no
doubt, find their voices, but unfortunate Catholic fami-
lies, owing to Oraunge atrocities, may starve. The
** Irish Times’’ interested itself in a fund for the victims
of the ‘“ Titanic '’; will it interest itself in a fund for
the vietims of many of the insensate bigots and black-
guards who helped to make the ‘* Titanic '’ ? Not likely.
What is it to the anti-Irish *“ Irish Times >’ that Catho-
lics may starve in Belfast.

What are the police doing in Belfast? We see that
resolutions have been passed d8manding the recall of
Mr. Commissioner Smith. Of five Inspectors-General,
three are English and two Irish; and of the five, four
are non-Catholics. ~ They are divided this way—The
I.-G., Protestant; the D.I.-G., Catholic, and three
A.I.G.’s, Protestants. The Commissioner of Belfast
is, almost needless to say, a Protestant. And all'this
in face of the fact that the strength of the rank and
file of the R.I.C. is about 10,500, of whom, approxi-
mately, 8,500 are Catholics and 2,000 non-Catholics.
The Inspector-General was appointed from the army in
South Africa in 1901, where he was private secretary to
Lord Roberts. His predecessor, Sir A. Reed, was then
serving and knew nothing of his retirement by the then
Irvish Government until he saw Col. Chamberlain’s
appointment in-the daily papers by wire from Pretoria,
South Africa.  Sir A. Reed was then in his prime.
But the fact that he favoured all the promotions from
the ranks gave great offence to the Tory Party. At
present no District-Inspector, promoted from the rank
of Head-Constable, is allowed to attain the rank of °
County Inspector. All such promotions are reserved
for cadet D.-I.’s.  All the R.I.C. officers, we believe,
are Tories to the core, with the exception of a few
Catholic officers promoted from the ranks.

As we pointed out a few weeks ago whilst discussing
this police bigotry, Mr. C. J. Foley, the County Inspec-
tor who tracked down the infamous Sheridan, was re-
tired the very day he completed his time, about twelv
months ago.. Immediately he was retired he made 8~
long statement to the Chief Secretary of his treatedént -
by the Inspector-General, and a question was séked
Parliament by Dr. Esmonde, to which the rsual red
tape reply was given. Now it was a giedt git.y that
the Irish Party, when Dr. Esmonde agked the Chief
Secretary about Mr. Foley’s written” statement . con-
cerning the Tnspecfor-General, did not press Mr. Bir-
rell to publish this statement as a White Paper for the
information of the House of Comm®ns and the country.
Is it too late now? We would like to see that state-
ment, and perhaps if we got it we would publish :it.
The Irish Party should press for the publication of that
statement as a White Paper. -

Mr. Michael Sullivan asked somé questions which
were not answered at the half-yearly meeting of the

Provincial Bank. He wanted information about the
examination for clerkships. Mr. Sullivan pointed out'
that the idea Lord Rathmore gave the shareholders was,

582

that from 1911 there would be: the ordinary’ examina-
tion of persons-nominated, and: that!the examinations
would be conductediby good ‘examiners: disconnected
with the bank. He desired to know how many exami-
nations had been conducted on that system for the last;
twelve months, and approximately how many competed
and how many got places. The chairman did nob give
Mr. Sullivan the information he asked for, but he stated
that no doubt they would be able to get it for him.
We look forward with great interest to the information
thg\t Mz, Sullivan may receive. By the way, a Mr.
Trings‘dragged in the religious denominational element
into' tlie case, and we believe he referred to Mr. Sul-
Ilyun as:Mr: Gore! The chairman remarked that they
did not'get the number of Catholic applicants that they
would like to'see, and the reason was, he thought, that
Catholics did not'go in for commereial life, but for pro-
fessions! If' the directors could get more Catholic
applications they would be- only too glad to have them:.
If the chaivman were only as smart ab assigning reasons
for financial phenomena as he is for the scarcity of
Catholic' applicants to' the bigot Provincial Bank we
would not think much of him as a banker. The Catho-
lics »did ‘not' go in for commerce, but for professions!
Did you: ever hear such undiluted twaddle! Of course,
the:Catholiecs who go in for professions are obviously
limited to the number who can afford to qualify for pro-
fessions, and that is a small number. Young men go
to be bank clerks who cannot afford to go into a profes-
sion, and have nothing better and softer to turn to than
bank clerking. Where do various other banks get their
‘““ Idolater *’ clerks? How is it that Papist clerks rush
into the Civil Service? How is it that Papists go into
commercial offices? The c that there is a shortage
of Catholic long-totters for the Provincial Banlk, be-
cause the Catholics go in for the professions is ab-
solutely puerile.

We welcome to the ranks of Irish Catholic Colleges
Coldiroe thuipe 1 nJaittnn—St. Mary’s College, Gal-
way. It will open on August 26th next, under the
patronage of His Lordship the Most Rev. Dr. O’Dea.
T'wenty-four scholarships, in each grade, value for
€212, will be open to boys from all Ireland, clerical
and lay. The buildings were designed by Mr. William
Scott, whose fame as an Irish architect is familiar to
our readers—we should call him Professor Scott now,
as he holds the Chair of Architecture in the National
University.  The Prospectus sa “ The formation
of Christian character will be regarded as incomparably
the most important part of the training. Religion
will, therefore, dominate the whole life of the College,
the aim being throughout to form in every boy the
habit of using his freedom rightly in all things, in
accordance with Christian principles Freedom will
accordingly be given, and self-discipline will be en-
couraged by trusting to the sense of honour of the
boys.”” Irish and Irish History are also emphasised in
the Prospectus.. “‘ Irish will be taught to all, and will
be spoken freely in the College, as it is everywhere in
the Galway district.  Irish Literature and History will
receive a place of honour, and the boys will be led to
take an interest in Irish problems, and always to give a
preference to Ivish products. Modern languages will
be taught by speaking, as well as by the -older
methods.”” There is accommodation in the College for
sixty boarders and sixty day pupils. A very neatly
turned out bi-lingual and illustrated prospectus and
other particulars may be had on application to Father
Peter Davis, President, St. Mary’s College, Galway.

To any proper end. 'Tis troubled e’er

And when the people turn up their nose

And looks:with condescending pity down
On those who'do discard him.

The august combination of Skeffy and Kettle and 3

Cruso is broken up. We have seen that Skeffy has
turned on Kettle, and now Cruso has been tried and

has been found wanting. In the course of the leading |

article in last week’s Wexford ‘“ Free Press,”’” which is 8.

now edited by Cruso, we read :— g
1

The friends of Woman Suffrage both here and in
England cannot find language strong enough to
condemn the two scandalous attacks on the Prime
Minister in Dublin by English supporters of the

Suffrage movement. Not only were the attacks v
murderous in themselves—the attempt to burn 4

down the theatre and the throwing of a hatchet at

the Prime Minister were no ordinary acts of mili- \

tancy—but they were in no degree understandable.
Mr. Asquith has pledged himself to abide by the
decision of the House of Commons on the Reform
Bill notwithstanding that he is by conviction 1
opposed to the Suffrage. He ought to remain free ?
from attack till he has broken his pledge. If this <
kind of thing goes on we believe that the cause of
Woman Suffrage, a just cause, will be damaged
beyond hope. Every real friend of the Suffrage
movement must endeavour to stop this madness.
The authors of these outrages are not Irishwomen
nor do they live in Ireland. We think it is the
plain duty of the Irish Women’s Franchise League
to condemn these outrages as it has already dis-
associated itself from them. If it does not do so
it will have the responsibility of having killed the
movement in Ireland. Senseless and murderous
fanaticism of this kind is not politics, and cannot
be condoned by decent-minded men or women
whether they be Suffragists or not.

to be expected that some irresponsible people would
take advantage of-the situation to indulge in * fun ’>
after their own fashion by baiting Sufivagettes. But if
it is not correct to hold Irish Suffers responsible for
the hatchet throwing, neither is it correct to hold the
public that are thoroughly disgusted with the virago
_ Suffers and the Skeffy gang for the conduct of corner-
boys who may throw stones or attempt physical
violence on Suffers engaged in carrying on a public
meeting. . The conduct of the crowd that compelled
Skeffy and some followers to retreat behind the bars
where the monkies are in the Zoo is not conduct that
will meet with the approval of the general public who
are simply contemptuously laughing at the Skeffyites.
These people, however eccentric they may be, are en-
titled to advocate votes for women in public, and,
though a certain amount of banter and heckling is to
be expected, they should not be treated to physical
violence or persistently denied a hearing. ~ If people are
against them they can listen and heckle, or else—which
would be the better way—stay away altogether. The
hatchet throwing, the atfitude” of the Suffers
generally to Home Rule, the antics and personnel of
some of the imitation Suffers in Dublin have dis-
gusted and irritated people, but so long as the members
of the Skeffy gang confine themselves to talking about
votes for women they are acting within their rights,
and their rights should be respected. If people like it,
and no doubt a good deal of sport may be obtained in
that way by youngsters, let youngsters heckle them
and banter them by all means, but the Suffers are
entitled to their say, and there should be nothing
but condemnation for anything like physical violence.

new and more virile Vigilance Committee. ‘ Tame’

use all the aggressiveness we can command.’

report of a meeting in'a place called Blenheim Castle
with the caption: ““ The Sun has Risen.” We would
go further and tell ““ Alf ’ that the sun will continue
to rise every morning. What fools those politicians be.

Wicklow that has come to stay and’ to develolm

Like boots in Carlow, spinning was an innovation in
Wicklow. But the fouudmn of the spinning industry
in Wicklow has not had to face the difficulties that My.
Governey had to surmount, when, with no experience
of bootmaking, he started the factory that is now an
established success. It was exultingly said years ago
by an énemy that the Gael was going with a vengeance :
we cannot yet, unfoxtunate]y, say thu(, the Gael is ve-
turning with a rush, but he is coming back in single
file and in twos and threes. Mr, McVann, senr., lefb
Treland when very young and spent his working life
until recently in the textile industry of Yorkshire. He
brought up a large family, and the dream of some of
that fz\mil_v, though born in Yorkshire, was to settle
down in the land of their fathers. Three of the boys
went into the textile industry and two into engineering.
They ave all fully trained. Mr. J. T. McVann, whois the

manager, won distinetion at the City of London Guild,
he won a County Council Scholarship of two years in
Leeds University, and an honours certificate in woollen
spinning from Yorkshire College, and was at one time
manager of Messrs. Baldwin’s, of Halifax, a name that
is big in the spinning world. And now the father and
five of the sons are settled in Ireland, and the hum of
hundreds of spinning spindles is heard for the first time
in Wicklow town. They have brought to Ireland more
than capital, they have brought what Ireland needs far
more than capital for her industrial development; they
have brought specialised skill and technical training.
The return to Ireland of skill and training in the persons
of Irish men, or men of Irish extraction, anxious to
live their lives in Ireland, savours of poetic restitu-
tion. We want more of it: for skilled Irishmen, or
skilled men of Irish extraction, anxious to live in Ire-
land, are our great industrial need.

Why did Messrs. McVann decide on Wicklow rather
than on any other town? The story is worth relating.
Two of the brothers were on holidays in the neighbour-
hood and cycled one day down to Wicklow.  They
dallied on the quay side to look at an old mill that was
in years gone by a corn mill. An old man struck up
a conversation with them. ** For God’s sake take that
mill,” said he, * an’ start an industry : there is nothing
for the girls to do.”” That was how Wicklow was
brought definitely into the matter, but, as we have
said, it was a long-cherished dream of the McVann's
to settie in Ireland and start a factory.

That old corn mill was purchased last year and is

now a busy hive of industry. Machinery to the value
of about four or five thousand pounds is working away,
and Mr. J. McVann, whois a skilled engineer—and by
the ¢ has two machines of his own invention in the
factory—put up every bit of it.
The mill started work in last February and there was
no fourish of trumpets about it. The McVann’s merely
took off their coats and got into the work at once, and
there was and is no ‘* canary glove ’ business about the
‘enterprise. It is patent to any observer who visits the
mill. that it has success and development written all
over it.

It was at a day’s notice that, on the morning of the
day of Asquith’s meeting, I joined a motor party in
Dublin, and went, or should T not rather say spun, down
to Wicklow. Irish cutlery has not exhausted the Irish
industrial energies of-Mr. John Gore: he is interested
in this Wicklow enterprise also, and it was' he who
arranged the visit. Mr. J. P. Fitzgerald, of 30 Lr. Abbey
Street, who represents the company commercially, and
AMy. Harrington, buyer of the worsteds in Todd Burns
and Co., also came down. The three, very consider-
ately for themselves, took the back seat, whilst T was
given the place of honour with the chaffeur in front—
something. substantial for the other-three to fall on
should we bash into anything! As Mr. Gore was pre-
siding genius, needless to say nothing was carried but
petrol and Irvish-made cigars. The company and the
journey were most interesting, but, however, T must not
forget that spinning in Wicklow is my subject.

Now for a little spinning ““ shop.”” The yarns spun
in the mill comprise every type used in the textile trade.

Tt is interesting to know that Wicklow is now spinning 2

yarn for all the Dublin poplin manufacturers.  This
poplin yarn probably is the finest and most finished class
of yarn that is made anywhere. It is made from the
finest wools, and the material passes through more
machines than any other textile yarn. ~After the yarn

is spun and made into_two-ply thread, it goes through -

u process that is specially interesting 10 a mere out-
sider. Practically perfect as it is, there are yet some

fluff or loose fibres to be dealt with before it is abso- -

lutely finished, and so the thread is passed through a

flame of gas to burn-off anything in the way of loose °

fibre. 1 saw the yarn going through the flames, other-
wise it would be incredible to me that the thread could
travel such a journey without being promptly burned.
The finishing of yarn for poplin requires long experience.
Previous to the founding of this Wicklow industry all
the poplin yarn, I understand, had to be imported.

From poplin to Ballyvourney is a big stretch. Dr. g

Lynch, as our readers know, es_tahlished. some years
ago a hosiery factory in *‘ the capital of Irish Ireland.””
It wust be doing well, judging by all the yarn that
Wicklow is spinning for it for machine knitting and
for ladies’ golf coats. I saw material gomg through
the machines for manufacture into carpet yarns for two
firms in or near Dublin, and *‘ fingering "’ wools in
black, white and mixtures, for a large wholesale house
in Dublin.  The City of Dublin Woollen Mills' take
large quantities of weaving yarns, and we were not
surprised at that, as Mr. McKnight, of that company,
is a very prominent figure in the industrial develop-
ment movement. Foxford and Convoy Woollen Mills,
and many of the wholesale Dublin houses, leave big
orders with the factory.

The number of departments in the factory is surpris-
ing for ‘a new industry, but, as. Mr. J. 'I'. McVann
pointed out to me, as they are competent and able,
owing to their training, to malke for every branch of the
trade, they thought it best to do so and not confine
themselves to only a few lines. There are the carding
and mixing department ; the drawing department, where
a full range of machines reduce the material by stages
prior to spinning ; the spinning department, where there
is every type of spinning frame used in the trade, a
patent spinning frame, having no less than 200 spindles,
was spinning fingering yarns here; the folding depart-
ment, in which are a number of long machines for
making yvarns into 2 or 4-ply; the reeling department,

where the yarn is made into 1 or 2 oz. skeins, according’ *

to the requirements of each trade; the winding depart-

ment, where a series of winding machines for hanks, . .-

tubes or parcel winding were at work; the fancy yam
department, where fancy loop yarn is made, a speciul
yarn made here by a machine invented by Mr. Joe
McVann.  Mr. Joe McVann also made a machine for
making loop yarns into 1 oz. or 2 oz. balls. Then
there are the finishing, dyeing and drying departments,
and, last of all, the making up department, where a
stafl of gitls put on small tickets on each skein of yaurn
indicating that it is of Trish manufacture.

Mr. John T. McVann is enthusiastic in praise of the
Wicklow gitls in the factory. ‘‘ I consider that these
gitls in Wicklow,”” said he to me, *‘ who never saw such
machinery in their lives, are far superior to those on
the other side. They learn in half the time.”” I had

a chat with the first girl that was employed'in the fac- -

tory. . *“ The best girl in the place,”” said My, McVann.
She can mind, any machine in the factory.” .
The factory overlooks Wicklow harbour and there is
rcom for the inevitable extension. Wicklow for spin-
ning ought shortly to come to the mind as does Bal-

briggan for hosiery. When buying cloth our readers

General Wolfe Tone then advanced to the rostrum

and a scene of indescribable enthusiasm was witnessed,
the entire audience rising to their feet and waving hand-
kerchiefs ‘and green flags. The cheering lasted fully
five minutes and a quarter. Someone started singing
‘“ Who Fears to Speak of '98?"’ and the song was at
once taken up by the whole house. Order having at
length been restored, General Wolfe Tone congratulated
his hearers on their National spirit. He would read to
them the resolution he had the honour to move :—

*“ Resolved—That this huge concourse of patriotic
Irishmen, in meeting assembled, desire to place
on record their solemn protest at the omission from
the Asquith meeting of the names of many of the
greatest patriots in Irish history, and at the ab-
sence of any allusion to their sacrifices for the
cause of Irish freedom; and that copies of this re-
solution be forwarded to Messrs. John K. Red-
mond, John Dillon, and Joseph Devlin.”’

That was a resolution which, in his opinion, needed
no words to recommend it. In a speech of considerable
length and moving eloquence, the speaker then pro-
ceeded to show why the resolution should be adopted.
He pointed out that he had what he might term a
special grievance. A memorial had been started to him
in Dublin over ten years ago and was not yet com-
pleted (cries.of ‘‘ shame ’’). It was a shame. Still
he had got used to that. But he was not used to the
omission of his name from National meetings (a voice—
“You're worthy of it.”’) It was all very well to say
that the mere mention of him and men like him would
embarrass the Government, but was it not due to them
that Ireland was as she was to-day? (Hear, hear,

A MEMORABLE SLAVIC GATHERING.
By R: J. Kervy, B.L.
FEW weeks ago there was held in Prague, the
picturesque capital of Bohemia, a “most

remarkable and memorable demonstration—one in its
numbers and in its component parts unique in every

respeet. It comprised representatives from all the
countries of South-Eastern Europe, and its inter-
national importance has already been sufficiently

recognised by the French and German Press, who
variously regard it from their different standpoints of
friendliness and distrust. Le Petit Parisien, one of the
largest circulating and most influential papers in
Trance, in a most interesting article written by its
representative, M. Cheradame, whom I met at. the
demonstration, dealt with the ‘situation ’” and gave
expression to the significance of the event, expressing
views which it occurs to me are a faithful representa-
tion of the actual state of facts as so revealed. It
spoke of this memorable meeting of the Slavs as reveal-
ing a ‘“ new Burope,” and such it has. The presence
on the one occasion of such different peoples as for the
first time met under the common bond of Slavie
solidarity and brotherhood, and came together from
widely separated countries as members of that great
and growing Slav race meant much to those who read
the signs of the times correctly. It was the first
occasion upon. which these hitherto disconnected
nationalities did orcould meet owing to circumstances of
oppression and disorganisation on a common platform
and for a common purpose. There we had Bulgarian
and Servian, Russian and Pole, Croatian and Mora-
vian, Dalmatian and Bosnian, Herzegovinian and
Ruthene, Bohemian and Montenegrin, all brought
together under the inspiring and expanding Sokol
Organisation. All gathered together, these young men
and women, to take part in the exercises and drill
movements, also to march in procession through the
town, filling its ample square, or more prominently and
actively to engage in the drill and gymnastic proceed-
ings that went on for three days within the spacious
grounds of the enclosed Stadium erected on the Letna
plain just outside the city. The extent of the vast con-
course of people there and then assembled may be gauged
by the fact that there was in this enclosed place seating
accommodation for over two hundred thousand persons
provided, and that not a seat was vacaut, while outside
and around was standing room. for nearly as many more
persons.  Inside on various occasions ten thousand
young men and six thousand women, at different times
and separately, went through all their drill evolutions
with admirable ease, gracefulness and decorum,
without any crowding, crushing, confusion or incon-
venience. And a wonderful sight it certainly was to
“see these 10,000 young men and 6,000 young women,
with-the rigid regularity of ‘clockwork and with the pre-
cision of a military machine, go through the varied
exercises prescribed. They formed and reformed into
squares, columns and lines,. and other formations, with
such attractive and surprising accuracy that to an on-

There was no crushing or crowding, no unseemly con-
duct, no disorderliness, drunkenness or riotous conduct.

I never in any country saw so many persons. gathered
together who behaved in a more decorous and seemly
manner, who conducted themselves so well. And in
that crowd some twenty different languages were being
spoken.  The approaches to the Ietna Plain were
thronged with eager crowds gathered from all parts
of South-Eastern Europe, for the trains each day and
for days before brought in their overflowing contingents.
The Sokol Organisation has now spread to all the Slav
countries, and it is their distinctive organisation. From
having been originally started in Bohemia, and being a
purely Bohemian. institution it has become inter-
national among the Slav peoples. It embraces coun-
tries apparently diverse and scparate, but still joined
by the one common bonc of Slavic nationality—such a_
tie, as in presence of the overmastering influence of
Pan-Germanism .and its apprehended aggressiveness,

The concerted reunion of these Sokol delegates,
Czech, Slovene, Slovae, Croatian, Serb, Bulgarian
and Montenegrin, signifies the moral mobilisation
of 50 millions of the inhabitants of Central Europe
and the Balkans who wish to have an influence
proportional to their numbers upon the destinies of
Iurope than that which they have at present. It
is for this reason that the manifestations at Prague
constituted a . considerable fact in the history of
the Continent.  The impression produced by
those Sokol fetes has been profound, Thanks to

The appeal is as follows:—
BELFAST EXPELLED WORKERS' RELIEF
FUND.
APPEAL TO THE CATHOLICS OF IRELAND.

WE, THE CATHOLICS OF BELFAST, in Public
Meeting assembled, appeal to the Catholics of Ire-
land to come to the aid of our persecuted brethren in
this City. It is not unknown to you how, af the
instigation of certain well-known political fire-
brands, the Unionist Clubs, called into being by
Lord Templetown, have fomented disturbances in
Belfast Shipyards and other public werls, leading
to the expulsion amid scenes of brutal violence of
almost 3,000 Catholic workingmen.  These repre-

TIE LEADER.

sent fully 8,000 persons who, at the present time,
are in the utmost necessity. Many of those ex-
pelled are natives of other parts of Ireland who
have come to Belfast to throw in their lot with its
citizens: hence the universality of this Appeal.

His Lordship, Most Rev. Dr. Tohill, Bishop of
Down and Connor, at the first meeting of the
Catholic Vigilance Committee, stated that, in call-
ing it together, he desired no political significance
to be attached to his action. This is-our present
attitude; our only object is the alleviation of dis- -
tress. We desire the support, moral and financial,
of the Catholics of this country to.enable us to
secure that every Catholic worker in this city and
neighbourhood shall have the right to work and the
protection of the Authorities while so doing. We
desire financial aid to assist us in keeping Catholic
homes together and in providing their inmates with
the necessaries of life, until the Authorities and
the Employers of Labour shall have restored law .
and order to the Shipyards, Workshops, and Fac- ©
tories of Belfast.

The Evicted Tenants were looked upon as the
*“ wounded soldiers '’ of the Land War, and were
maintained by a National Subscription until the
advent of happier times: the men evicted, amid cir-
cumstances of such ferocity, from the Shipyards

religious persuasion; for, unhappily, some hun-
dreds of noble Protestant workmen are to-day
walking the streets of Belfast, expelled, along with
their Catholic brethren, for no other crinte than that
of daring to have aspirations in harmony with those
of the majority of their fellow-countrymen.
